findings.reachability: Runs automatically for any authenticated admin on the troubleshooting settings page (via localizeSettings() at line 207). The DB row must first be poisoned — requires write access to the WBW Product Filter settings table (shop-admin level or a compromised WBW plugin).
findings.justification: unserialize($filter['setting_data']) is called on data fetched from a third-party plugin's database table with no allowed_classes restriction; a poisoned serialized payload in that table triggers object injection during every admin page load without any AJAX call.

detection_pattern.description: Matches any call to unserialize() that does not specify the allowed_classes option. PHP unserialize without allowed_classes can trigger __wakeup/__destruct on arbitrary classes present in the codebase (POP chain), making it exploitable whenever the serialized input originates from DB rows, options, or transients written by users or other plugins.

findings.reachability: Runs when the async troubleshooting test 'LoopbackRequests' is executed (authenticated admin, wp_ajax_dgwt_wcas_troubleshooting_test with nonce). Requires the inbound request to carry HTTP Basic Auth headers.
findings.justification: Helpers::getBasicAuthHeader() extracts PHP_AUTH_USER and PHP_AUTH_PW from the inbound request's Authorization header and re-forwards them as the Authorization header in the outbound wp_remote_get() call to home_url(); wp_remote_get() follows redirects by default, so any open-redirect at home_url() could relay the credentials to an attacker-controlled host.

detection_pattern.section_name: Credential Forwarding: Basic Auth headers echoed to outbound HTTP request
detection_pattern.description: Matches code that reads PHP_AUTH_USER or PHP_AUTH_PW from $_SERVER (the inbound Basic Auth credentials) to construct an Authorization header for an outbound HTTP request. If the target URL follows redirects or is partly attacker-controlled, the site's own admin credentials can be exfiltrated.
